codingTopics: Practical Go: parsers, queues, small state machines
Streaming and time-series: windowed aggregation over metrics
String parsing: query languages (PromQL-style), log lines
Data structures: ring buffers, LRU caches for query results
Graph and topological: dependency of query execution stages
systemDesignTopics: Design a time-series database with horizontal scaling and a strict query SLA
Design a distributed log aggregation system ingesting 100TB per day
Design a metrics query engine with a PromQL-style language and pushdown optimization
Design distributed tracing storage and retrieval across services

coding: Parse a stream of Prometheus-style metric lines (name{labels} value timestamp) and aggregate by label set over a sliding window. Discuss memory.
Implement a rate-limited, back-pressured queue for log ingestion in Go. Discuss what happens when a downstream sink is slow.
Given a time-series of samples, downsample to fixed buckets (min, max, avg per bucket) as data streams in. Walk through the approach.
Parse and evaluate a simple PromQL-like expression (selector plus range plus aggregation) against an in-memory series store.
behavioral: Grafana is fully remote and async. Tell me about a time strong written communication unblocked a distributed team.
Describe an open-source contribution or public work you're proud of and the tradeoffs you made.
Give an example of a practical, pragmatic fix you chose over an elegant but heavy solution.
Tell me about a time you debugged a hard production issue in a distributed system.
systemDesign: Design a time-series database that scales horizontally and holds a 99.9% query SLA.
Design a distributed log aggregation system ingesting 100TB per day (the Loki problem).
Design a metrics query engine with a PromQL-style language and pushdown optimization.
values: Grafana is deeply open-source. How do you weigh community and upstream needs against a single customer's request?
Describe a time you made your work legible to others so they could build on it.
How do you operate effectively without synchronous meetings or a manager watching over you?

rawSummary:
Grafana Labs Interview Guide (2026): Observability Engineering
By 			
				
				techinterview.org			
			
		

		 /  May 5, 2026 
			
		

	
	
	

		
		
On this page

- Process

- What they actually ask

- Levels and comp (2026)

- Prep priorities

- Frequently Asked Questions

Updated May 5, 2026 · techinterview.org

⏱ 1 min read

Grafana Labs
grafana.com ↗

Grafana Labs builds the LGTM stack — Loki (logs), Grafana (visualization), Tempo (traces), Mimir (metrics). The company is fully remote, deeply open-source, and the interview process reflects that: practical, async-friendly, and skeptical of pure LeetCode theatrics.

Process

Recruiter screen → 60-minute technical phone (often pair-coding on a small task) → onsite virtual loop: 1 coding (pair, often Go), 1 system design (always observability-flavored), 1 craft deep-dive, 1 behavioral, 1 hiring manager. Cycle: 3–5 weeks (slow because everyone is distributed).

What they actually ask

- Design a time-series database with horizontal scaling and 99.9% query SLA

- Design a distributed log aggregation system handling 100TB/day ingestion

- Design a metrics query engine with PromQL-style language and pushdown optimization

- Coding: practical problems in Go — parsers, queues, simple state machines

- Open source contribution review: be ready to walk through any past OSS work

Levels and comp (2026)

- SE II: $160K–$200K total

- Senior SE: $230K–$300K

- Staff: $330K–$430K

- Principal: $450K–$580K

Prep priorities

- Be fluent in Go — it is the lingua franca of the LGTM stack

- Understand columnar storage, time-series compression, and Prometheus internals

- Demonstrate genuine OSS engagement — issues, PRs, blog posts all count

Frequently Asked Questions

Is Grafana Labs really fully remote?

Yes. Hubs in NYC, London, Stockholm, Sydney exist but are optional. 600+ employees in 50+ countries.

Do I need observability experience?

Not strictly. They hire generalists who are excited to learn. Curiosity about Prometheus or having read their docs goes a long way.

How does Grafana Labs compare to Datadog?

Datadog pays more cash but is closed-source SaaS. Grafana is OSS-first, more remote-friendly, and has strong "build in public" culture.
